agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with deliberately vague language ('probably', 'competitive but also profitable', 'don't go crazy'), indicating this is an early-stage concept without formal specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current delivery request volume relative to some baseline or capacity — definition is unspecified" },
      { "term": "Delivery Price", "definition": "The price charged to a customer for a delivery, subject to dynamic adjustment" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Real-time adjustment", "definition": "Price changes that occur automatically in response to current demand conditions without human intervention" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. The direction and magnitude of price response to demand is a business decision that leadership could change (e.g., they might later want to cap surge pricing or flatten the curve). Not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the 'high' threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a pricing policy direction. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead of discounting.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand falls below the 'low' threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur automatically without manual human intervention",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a product requirement, not a state integrity constraint. The business could introduce manual overrides or approval workflows later.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand level, the system adjusts prices without requiring any human approval step"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A price ceiling (upper guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum acceptable amount",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a price ceiling, the system could charge arbitrarily high prices, creating legal liability (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), reputational damage, and customer harm. This is a safety constraint — the system is corrupt if it can produce unbounded prices.",
      "testable_assertion": "No dynamically computed delivery price may ever exceed the configured maximum price ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price floor (lower guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from falling below a minimum acceptable amount",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a price floor, the system could price deliveries at zero or negative values, violating basic financial integrity. A delivery always has a non-negative cost; pricing below that is system corruption, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "No dynamically computed delivery price may ever fall below the configured minimum price floor, and the floor must be greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Price must be a positive monetary value at all times",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery price of zero or negative is physically and financially nonsensical. This is a data integrity constraint — the system state is corrupt if a non-positive price is produced.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every computed delivery price must be strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be based on a measurable demand signal as its primary input",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The choice of demand signal (order volume, courier availability, geographic density, etc.) is a product/analytics decision that will evolve. Not a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price computation function accepts at least one demand metric as input and the output price varies as that metric changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must reflect current (real-time) demand, not stale or historical demand alone",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The freshness window for 'real-time' is a policy decision (seconds? minutes? 15-minute rolling average?). A VP could change the window. But the intent is clear: prices should track current conditions.",
      "testable_assertion": "The demand signal used for price computation must have been measured within a configured recency window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness (not overpricing) with profitability (not underpricing)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic objective, not a hard constraint. The tension between competitive and profitable is resolved by policy parameters (margins, multipliers) that leadership tunes.",
      "testable_assertion": "NOT DIRECTLY TESTABLE — this is a strategic goal that must be decomposed into specific margin/threshold rules before it becomes testable"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 10x normal) in seconds", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail must cap the price; the system must not produce prices above the ceiling regardless of demand magnitude" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero (no delivery requests)", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail must prevent price from reaching zero or negative; system must remain stable and produce valid prices" },
    { "scenario": "Demand signal becomes unavailable or stale (e.g., telemetry failure)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — CEO description does not address fallback behavior. Must be specified: does system freeze last known price? Revert to base price? Alert ops?" },
    { "scenario": "Two simultaneous requests see different demand levels due to race condition", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — CEO description does not address consistency model. Must be specified: is eventual consistency acceptable or must all concurrent requests see the same price?" },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values are misconfigured (e.g., floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configuration and refuse to operate with contradictory bounds" },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation causing price to flip between high and low repeatedly", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no mention of smoothing, dampening, or rate-of-change limits. Without these, customers may see wildly fluctuating prices within minutes." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory jurisdiction with anti-price-gouging laws during declared emergencies", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — CEO does not address legal/regulatory constraints that may impose external price ceilings stricter than business guardrails" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing is a core revenue lever. Correct implementation improves margins during peak demand and maintains volume during troughs. Incorrect implementation risks revenue leakage (floor too low) or demand destruction (ceiling too high/missing).",
    "operational": "Fully automated system removes human pricing decisions, reducing ops overhead but creating dependency on system reliability. Monitoring and alerting become critical operational concerns.",
    "user": "Customers experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price perception is a major driver of customer satisfaction in delivery services.",
    "risk": "HIGH — price gouging liability in regulated markets, reputational risk from extreme prices, revenue risk from pricing bugs, competitive risk if prices are not responsive enough. The vagueness of 'probably have some guardrails' is a red flag.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Directly affected by price changes; may perceive unfairness during high-demand periods; need transparency" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Pricing directly affects revenue forecasting, margin targets, and financial planning" },
      { "group": "Operations team", "impact": "Must monitor automated system; need alerting for anomalies; lose manual pricing control" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and disclosure requirements" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If courier compensation is tied to delivery price, dynamic pricing affects their earnings" },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships", "impact": "Enterprise or partner contracts may have fixed-price agreements that conflict with dynamic pricing" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a specification. Nearly every critical parameter is undefined: what constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand, what the guardrail values are, what 'real-time' means quantitatively, how competitiveness vs profitability is balanced, what the demand signal is, and whether guardrails are even confirmed ('probably'). Multiple rules extracted are inferences from vague language. This is pre-spec ideation, not a reviewable requirement.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)",
      "Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'demand' — no metric specified",
      "Guardrail values (ceiling and floor) — not defined at all",
      "What 'real-time' means in practice — latency tolerance unspecified",
      "How competitiveness vs profitability is balanced — no formula or even directional guidance",
      "Fallback behavior when demand signal is unavailable",
      "Whether pricing applies uniformly or varies by geography, customer segment, or delivery type",
      "Rate-of-change limits or smoothing on price adjustments",
      "Legal and regulatory compliance requirements",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor (CEO only said 'guardrails' generically)",
      "A delivery price must always be positive (basic financial integrity, not stated)",
      "The demand signal is measurable and available in near-real-time",
      "Dynamic pricing applies to the customer-facing delivery fee"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The word 'demand' is never defined. It could mean: (A) order volume per time window, (B) order volume relative to courier supply, (C) geographic density of requests, (D) some composite metric. The entire pricing model depends on this definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Revenue team", "Operations team", "Data/Analytics team"],
      "options": ["Define demand as order volume per time window", "Define demand as order-to-courier ratio (supply/demand)", "Define demand as a composite index"],
      "recommendation": "Order-to-courier ratio is the most meaningful metric in logistics — raw volume without supply context produces incorrect pricing signals. Recommend (B) but need product/data team confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail values (price ceiling and floor) are not specified. The CEO hedged with 'probably.' Without concrete values, the most critical safety constraint is untestable.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Revenue team"],
      "options": ["Define guardrails as absolute dollar amounts", "Define guardrails as multipliers of base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3x)", "Define guardrails as percentage deviations from base price"],
      "recommendation": "Multiplier-based guardrails (e.g., floor = 0.7x base, ceiling = 2.5x base) are industry standard for surge pricing. Recommend multiplier approach with specific values to be set by Finance and approved by Legal."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real-time' is undefined. This could mean sub-second, per-minute, per-5-minutes, or per-15-minute recalculation windows. The freshness of the demand signal materially affects pricing accuracy and system architecture.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Sub-second (per-request pricing)", "Per-minute batch recalculation", "Configurable window with a default"],
      "recommendation": "Per-minute recalculation with configurable window. Sub-second is architecturally expensive and unlikely needed; anything over 5 minutes undermines the 'real-time' claim."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior is defined for when the demand signal is unavailable (telemetry failure, data pipeline lag). An automated system with no fallback could either freeze at last-known prices (potentially stale and wrong) or fail to price entirely (blocking all orders).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations team", "Finance/Revenue team"],
      "options": ["Revert to base price on signal loss", "Freeze last known price with time-based expiry", "Circuit-breaker pattern: revert to base after N seconds of stale data"],
      "recommendation": "Circuit-breaker with revert to base price after configurable staleness threshold. This is a safety-critical design decision."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of whether dynamic pricing applies uniformly or is segmented by geography, customer tier, delivery type, time of day, or other dimensions. In logistics, these segments have materially different demand patterns and price sensitivities.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales/Partnerships", "Finance/Revenue team"],
      "options": ["Single global pricing model", "Geographic zone-based pricing", "Multi-dimensional segmentation"],
      "recommendation": "At minimum, geographic zone-based pricing. A single global model will produce nonsensical prices in a multi-market delivery business."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Legal and regulatory review has not been mentioned. Dynamic pricing / surge pricing is regulated in multiple jurisdictions (price gouging laws, consumer protection acts, transparency requirements). Shipping without legal review is a compliance risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Proceed with engineering, defer legal review", "Block implementation until legal review completes", "Proceed with conservative guardrails and mandatory legal review before launch"],
      "recommendation": "Proceed with implementation using conservative guardrails, but BLOCK launch until Legal/Compliance reviews and approves guardrail values and customer-facing transparency requirements."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
